examples.en: I am a student.
This ramen is famous.

connection: N / na-adj + じゃありません
nuance: じゃありません is the casual-polite form; ではありません is the more formal equivalent.

examples.en: I am not a shop clerk.
I'm not free today.

nuance: As a topic particle は is pronounced "wa", not "ha".

examples.en: The share house is quiet.
I take the train every morning.

nuance: が marks the grammatical subject, often new or emphasized info; は marks the topic.

examples.en: There is a konbini in front of the station.
Which train is faster?

nuance: As a particle を is pronounced "o".

examples.en: I buy tea at the konbini.
I study Japanese every night.

connection: time N + に / place N + に
nuance: に marks a point in time or a destination; で marks the place an action happens.

examples.en: Work starts at seven o'clock.
On Sunday I go to the sento.

connection: place N + で / tool N + で
nuance: で marks where an action takes place, or the means/tool used to do it.

examples.en: I drink with friends at the izakaya.
I went to the ward office by bus.

nuance: As a direction particle へ is pronounced "e".

examples.en: I want to go to Hokkaido.
Next week I'll climb Mt. Fuji.

nuance: と lists items exhaustively ("A and B, that's all"); や lists a few examples.

examples.en: I'll go to the konbini and the supermarket.
I drank at an izakaya with a friend.

nuance: や implies "A and B, among others" — the list is not complete.

examples.en: There's tea, milk and such in the fridge.
In Akihabara I looked at manga, games and the like.
My room is on the second floor.
I bought a Japanese book.

nuance: も replaces は or が (not just adds to them) to mean "also / too".

examples.en: I like the sento too.
Tomorrow is an early shift too.

nuance: か at the end of a sentence turns it into a question; no question mark needed.

examples.en: Where is the toilet?
Does this train go to Shinjuku?

nuance: ね seeks agreement or shared feeling — "right?", "isn't it?".

examples.en: It's hot today, isn't it?
This ramen is delicious, isn't it?

nuance: よ tells the listener something new or emphasizes your point — "you know", "I'm telling you".

examples.en: The last train has already gone, you know.
That izakaya over there is cheap, you know.
I drink coffee every morning.
I'll meet a friend tomorrow.
I won't drink alcohol today.
I don't eat natto.
I went to the ward office yesterday.
I climbed Mt. Fuji last week.
I didn't go to work yesterday.
I didn't make the last train.

nuance: The plain (casual) negative; ない conjugates like an い-adjective.

examples.en: I'm not going anywhere today.
I don't eat anything in the morning.
I didn't sleep yesterday.
I didn't take an umbrella with me.

nuance: Expresses an action in progress or a resulting state; casual いる, polite います.

examples.en: I'm waiting for the train now.
I'm studying Japanese every night.
Please wait here.
Please say it one more time.
Please don't take photos here.
Please don't make phone calls on the train.
Let's eat ramen together.
Let's head home now.
Shall I open the window?
Where shall we meet?

nuance: A softer, more polite invitation than ましょう — "would you like to…?".

examples.en: Won't you go to an izakaya tonight?
Would you like to go to a sento this weekend?

meaning: do things like A and B
connection: V-たり + V-たり + する
nuance: Lists representative actions, not a full or ordered sequence — "do things like A and B".

examples.en: On my day off I do things like cleaning and laundry.
On weekends I do things like watch anime and read manga.

connection: V-ない stem + なければなりません
nuance: Drop the ない and add なければなりません. Casual short form: なきゃ.

examples.en: I have to get up early tomorrow.
I have to submit documents at the ward office.

connection: V-ない stem + なくてもいい
nuance: Drop the ない and add なくてもいい to say something isn't necessary.

examples.en: I don't have to go to work today.
You don't have to take off your shoes.
I can read a little kanji.
You can withdraw money at the konbini.

nuance: の nominalizes the verb so it can be the thing you like; 好き is a na-adjective.

examples.en: I like going to the sento.
I like reading books on the train.

nuance: 上手 (skilled) is a na-adjective; usually used to praise others, not yourself.

examples.en: He is good at cooking.
My roommate is good at speaking Japanese.

nuance: 欲しい is for wanting THINGS; use 〜たい for wanting to DO something.

examples.en: I want a new bicycle.
In winter I want a warm coat.

nuance: Attaches to a verb stem or an adjective stem (drop final い/な); means "too" / "over-".

examples.en: This ramen is too spicy.
I drank too much at the izakaya yesterday.

nuance: Adds an explanatory nuance — giving a reason or background. のです is the formal form.

examples.en: It's that I have a headache.
The thing is, I missed the last train.

nuance: The clause before と思います stays in plain form. Some lists place this at N4; the lower level wins.

examples.en: I think it will be sunny tomorrow.
I think this shop is cheap.

nuance: Expresses probability or a guess; with rising intonation it seeks confirmation.

examples.en: It will probably rain tomorrow.
Hokkaido is probably cold.

connection: まだ + V-て + いません
nuance: まだ pairs with the negative ていません to mean "not yet (done)".

examples.en: I haven't eaten lunch yet.
I haven't gotten my residence card yet.

nuance: もう pairs with the past ました to mean "already (done)".

examples.en: I've already eaten dinner.
I've already opened a bank account.
It takes about ten minutes to the station.
I save about 30,000 yen every month.

nuance: ごろ attaches to a POINT in time (7時ごろ); ぐらい attaches to an amount (10分ぐらい).

examples.en: I get up around seven every morning.
The train comes around eight thirty.
Today I only bought water.
I can only read hiragana.

nuance: しか always pairs with a NEGATIVE verb but the meaning is positive-limiting: "only".

examples.en: I only have 100 yen in my wallet.
I can only take Sundays off.

connection: A + より + B + のほうが

examples.en: The bicycle is faster than the train.
I like winter more than summer.

connection: group + の中で + N + がいちばん

examples.en: Of all the cities in Japan, I like Tokyo the most.
At this restaurant, the ramen is the cheapest.
When I have free time, I go to the sento.
When I came to Japan, I went to the ward office.

nuance: The verb before あとで is always PAST plain form (食べたあとで), even in a present-tense sentence.

examples.en: After work is over, I go to the izakaya.
After eating, I wash the dishes.
Please tell me how to buy a ticket.
The way to write kanji is difficult.
I'm going to the konbini to buy a bento.
I'm going to Hokkaido to ski.
It's not very cold today.
I don't eat natto very much.
I don't understand this kanji at all.
I couldn't sleep at all last night.
How about the sento this weekend?
How about a coffee?
Please give me this one.
Two draft beers, please.

connection: い-adj stem + くなります

examples.en: At night it gets cold.
Japanese has gradually become fun.
Next month I turn 25.
I practiced every day and got good at it.

nuance: の turns a verb into a noun, like adding "-ing" or "the act of" (泳ぐ → 泳ぐの).

examples.en: I like reading books on the train.
Getting up early in the morning is tough.
This ramen is cheap and delicious.
The share house is spacious and bright.
I'll have the miso ramen.
For a drink, I'll have tea.
I wonder if it will rain tomorrow.
I wonder if this train stops at Shibuya.

nuance: Describes doing the main action WITHOUT the first one (朝ご飯を食べないで出た).

examples.en: I left home without eating breakfast.
I went out without taking an umbrella.

nuance: けど is the casual, softer counterpart of が; common in everyday speech.

examples.en: Natto is smelly, but it's delicious.
I want to go, but I don't have time.

nuance: This が is the contrastive conjunction ("but"), not the subject particle が.

examples.en: This place is expensive, but it's delicious.
Excuse me, but where is the station?
But I don't have any money today.
But it's raining, so I won't go.
I went to the sento. And then I drank milk.
I cleaned. And then I did laundry too.
I went to the ward office. After that, I went to the bank.
I bought bread. And also I bought eggs.
Tomorrow is work. That's why I'll go to bed early tonight.
It's cold outside. So I'll wear a coat.
I went to an izakaya together with a friend.
I'll climb Mt. Fuji together with my roommate.
I go to the station and buy a ticket.
In the morning I get up and wash my face.
What kind of music do you like?
What kind of room are you looking for?
After work ends, I do the shopping at the supermarket.
After arriving in Japan, I went to the ward office first.

connection: V-dict + 前に / N + の + 前に
nuance: The verb before 前に is ALWAYS dictionary form, even when the sentence is past tense.

examples.en: Before eating, I wash my hands.
Before going to bed, I check tomorrow's weather.

nuance: With a negative verb it flips to "neither … nor".

examples.en: Both the konbini and the supermarket are close by.
I study both kanji and katakana every day.

connection: V-ます stem + たい(です)
nuance: たい conjugates like an い-adjective: 食べたくない, 食べたかった.

examples.en: I want to eat ramen in Hokkaido.
I want to get good at Japanese.

connection: N + が + あります (things) / います (people & animals)
nuance: あります for inanimate things; います for people and animals.

examples.en: There is a sento near the station.
There is a cat in the share house.
May I take a photo here?
Is it OK to use the kitchen?
You must not talk on the phone inside the train.
You must not park bicycles here.

nuance: The ながら action is the secondary one; the main action comes second.

examples.en: I cook while listening to music.
I study Japanese while watching anime.

connection: V-た + ことがあります

examples.en: I have seen Mt. Fuji before.
I have been in the hot springs at Asahidake.

meaning: had better / it'd be better to
connection: V-た + ほうがいい(です); negative advice = V-ない + ほうがいい
nuance: Affirmative advice uses the PAST form (寝た), negative uses ない (寝ないほうがいい).

examples.en: You'd better take an umbrella.
You'd better go to bed early.

connection: plain or polite clause + から
nuance: Don't confuse with てから (after doing) — this から attaches to a reason clause.

examples.en: It's hot, so please open the window.
Tomorrow is an early start, so I'm going to bed now.
I plan to go to Hokkaido over the summer holiday.
I intend to still be in Japan next year.
